title: AMD And Nvidia Are Neck In HPC Supercomputing
gist: China's new 'LineShine' supercomputer at NSC Shenzhen — built on Huawei/HiSilicon's LingKun LX2 ARM-compatible CPU with 13.79 million cores — achieved 2.2 exaflops on HPL, surpassing the US El Capitan system by 21.5% and returning China to the top of the Top500 list. In the overall June 2026 Top500 rankings, AMD's installed accelerated HPC capacity (8.18 exaflops) is narrowing on Nvidia's (11 exaflops), suggesting a genuine race rather than Nvidia dominance.

insights.claim: China's 'LineShine' supercomputer at NSC Shenzhen, built on Huawei/HiSilicon's LingKun LX2 ARMv9 CPU with 13.79 million cores, achieved 2.2 exaflops on HPL — making it 21.5% more powerful than the previously top-ranked US system El Capitan (AMD MI300A at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory).
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: China's return to the top of the Top500 with fully indigenous CPU technology demonstrates that US export controls on GPU/accelerator chips have accelerated, not stalled, China's development of homegrown HPC architectures.
insights.quote: LineShine delivers just a tad under 2.2 exaflops of oomph and that makes it 21.5 percent more powerful than the former top ranked machine, the 'El Capitan' supercomputer

insights.claim: The LingKun LX2 CPU achieves competitive exascale performance with 304 active cores per chip using SVE2 vector units on an ARMv9-compatible architecture, demonstrating China's ability to design high-core-count server CPUs without access to Nvidia GPUs or advanced US-origin accelerators.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A 304-core ARMv9 server CPU achieving exascale FP64 performance is a strategic inflection point in China's semiconductor independence — it undermines the premise that export controls on GPU chips are sufficient to constrain China's HPC capability.
insights.quote: The LingKun LX2 CPU design has 304 active cores

insights.claim: China almost certainly has multiple additional exascale machines not officially submitted to Top500 — including the 1.5 exaflops OceanLight (NSC Wuxi, Sunway SW26010-Pro) and 2.05 exaflops Tianhe-3A (NSC Guangzhou) — meaning China's known total exascale installed base exceeds what the Top500 rankings show.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The true gap between US and Chinese HPC capacity is likely understated by official Top500 data; policymakers and defense analysts should treat China's de facto HPC capability as materially larger than the official list suggests.
insights.quote: China has been ahead in the exascale race even if it did not submit official Top500 results

themes.theme: AMD vs. Nvidia HPC Market Share

insights.claim: In the June 2026 Top500, AMD has 32 accelerated HPC systems with 8.18 exaflops of peak installed capacity versus Nvidia's 237 systems with 11 exaflops — meaning AMD's 12% machine-count share translates to 43% of Nvidia's flops capacity, a ratio that reflects AMD's concentration in large flagship systems.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: AMD's HPC accelerator position is far stronger than machine-count share suggests; the AMD MI300A's presence in the two largest Western machines (El Capitan, Eni HPC7) gives AMD outsize influence in defining the next generation of HPC architecture.
insights.quote: AMD has 8.18 exaflops of installed capacity compared to Nvdia's 11 exaflops, and in terms of concurrency, AMD has sold accelerated machines with a combine CPU and GPU concurrency of 35.3 million cores compared to Nvidia's 38.9 million cores. This is a real race

insights.claim: Many HPC centers on the June 2026 Top500 list chose older Nvidia Hopper (H100/H200) GPUs over newer Blackwell (B200/B300), citing Hopper's higher FP64 flops-per-dollar ratio — suggesting Blackwell's value proposition for traditional HPC (as opposed to AI training) is weaker than its marketing implies.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Blackwell GPU adoption in HPC may lag AI training deployments if FP64 performance-per-dollar favors Hopper; this creates a bifurcated product cycle where Nvidia's newest generation is not the preferred choice in a significant compute segment.
insights.quote: Hopper GPUs are cheaper, and they also have more FP64 flops and more flops per dollar than the follow-on 'Blackwell' B200 and B300 GPUs.

themes.theme: HPC Budget and Performance Growth Slowdown

insights.claim: HPC supercomputing capacity growth has fallen off the Moore's Law pace of doubling every two years — and the author attributes this explicitly to insufficient spending rather than technology constraints.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: HPC underinvestment relative to AI datacenter spending creates a policy gap; public HPC funding increases would disproportionately benefit AMD (dominant in flagship machines) and Nvidia HPC GPU lines.
insights.quote: we have still fallen off the Moore's Law wagon of doubling performance every two years, and at least in supercomputing, we are not spending enough money to get passage on the wagon. Take a look: This is a budgetary thing much more than it is a technology thing.

insights.claim: China's strategy for achieving HPC capability despite accelerator export controls is the all-CPU scale-out model — using MPI across massive homegrown CPU clusters rather than GPU/accelerator offload, with LineShine's 13.79 million cores delivering exascale performance without any Nvidia or AMD accelerators.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: China's CPU-centric exascale strategy is a proof point that export controls create alternative architecture innovation, not capability denial; this has implications for the medium-term effectiveness of US technology controls as a strategic tool.
insights.quote: Necessity is always the mother of invention... China can make a big machine that is relatively simple whose practical limits are space and power.

insights.claim: The Eni HPC7 supercomputer in Italy — ranked 6th globally — is based on AMD MI300A hybrid CPU-GPU accelerators, making it the largest commercial supercomputer with submitted Top500 results, demonstrating that the MI300A architecture has commercial viability beyond its US national laboratory deployments.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Eni's adoption of MI300A for oil and gas HPC signals that AMD's hybrid CPU-GPU approach has commercial traction in industrial HPC beyond the defense/national lab sector — a potential revenue diversification signal for AMD.
insights.quote: the HPC7 system at Italian oil and gas giant Eni, which is based on AMD's hybrid CPU-GPU MI300A accelerator; HPC7 is essentially a chip off the El Capitan block, and is ranked number six on the list. It is the largest commercial supercomputer with submitted results.
